claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask about the child (or children) you're applying for:**

1. **How old is the child?** (Or if you have multiple children, please tell me their ages)

The age of the child is important because:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (if other conditions are met)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please share the age(s) of your child/children, and we'll work through the eligibility requirements together.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello, yes I'd like to find out about Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 17 years old. I just want to check if I'm eligible to claim for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is 17 (which falls in the 16-19 age range), their eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to know about Alex's education or training status:

**Is Alex currently in education or training, or have they recently left education?**

Please tell me which of these applies:
- **Currently in education or training** (e.g., doing A-levels, Scottish Highers, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, or approved vocational training)
- **Recently left education** (within the last few months)
- **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

What's Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in education - they're studying for their A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is studying for A-levels at sixth form college, they are in approved education. Now I need to verify that the education level qualifies for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are definitely approved (non-advanced) education for Child Benefit purposes. Now I need to check a couple more things about Alex's education:

**1. Is Alex's education full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study - sixth form A-levels typically count as full-time)

**2. Is this education funded by an employer or linked to a job that Alex has agreed to take in the future?**

For most sixth form college students doing A-levels, the answers are typically "yes, it's full-time" and "no, it's not employer-funded." Can you confirm this for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - it's full-time at the sixth form college, just normal A-levels. And no, there's no employer involved or anything like that. Alex is just a regular student.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! A-levels at sixth form college definitely qualify as approved full-time education. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about Alex's work situation:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

For Child Benefit purposes, if a young person aged 16 or over works 24 hours or more per week, they may not be eligible. What's Alex's situation regarding paid work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - just focused on the studies at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is just focused on their studies with no paid work, so that's fine for Child Benefit purposes. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about benefits:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?**

Most students at sixth form college don't claim these benefits, but I need to confirm that Alex is NOT receiving ESA or Universal Credit in their own name.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits - they're just a student living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own right. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex doing an apprenticeship?**

You mentioned Alex is doing A-levels at sixth form college, which sounds like regular education rather than an apprenticeship. Can you confirm that Alex is NOT in an apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - Alex is not doing an apprenticeship, just the regular A-levels at sixth form.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is doing regular A-levels, not an apprenticeship. Let me continue to the next part of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we're moving on to check your eligibility as the claimant. The first requirement is about residency.

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

This is the main residency requirement. If you normally live in the UK, you'll meet this requirement. There are also special circumstances that can qualify (such as being a Crown servant abroad, or having settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme), but for most people, it's simply about normally living in the UK.

What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You normally live in the UK (in Manchester), so you meet the residency requirement. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check about your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You mentioned that Alex lives at home with you, which means you meet this requirement. Responsibility can be shown by:
- Living with the child (which you do), OR
- Contributing to their upkeep (paying for their care, food, clothes, etc.)

Since Alex lives at home with you in Manchester, you clearly meet this requirement. Can you confirm that's correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives here at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You live with Alex, so you're definitely responsible for them. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is claiming but you've agreed that you should claim instead, that can be arranged. What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about Alex's living situation.

**Is Alex currently:**
- In hospital (as an inpatient)?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Living normally (at home with you)?

You mentioned Alex lives at home with you, so I believe it's "living normally," but I need to confirm there are no special circumstances like hospital stays or care arrangements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is just living normally at home with me - no hospital, care, or fostering or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living normally at home with you. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just one final check:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

Since Alex lives at home with you and you're supporting them, I expect the answer is "no, the council is not paying." Can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Based on all the information you've provided, let me now navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE** ✓

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex.

### Why You're Eligible:

- ✓ **Alex qualifies as a dependent child** - At 17 years old and studying full-time A-levels at sixth form college, Alex meets the education requirements for 16-19 year olds
- ✓ **Alex is in approved education** - A-levels are non-advanced, approved qualifications
- ✓ **Alex is not working full-time** - Not working at all, so under the 24 hours/week limit
- ✓ **Alex doesn't receive benefits** - Not claiming ESA or Universal Credit
- ✓ **Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship** - Doing regular A-levels
- ✓ **You meet residency requirements** - You normally live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✓ **You're responsible for Alex** - Alex lives at home with you
- ✓ **No competing claims** - Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✓ **Normal living arrangements** - Alex lives at home, not in hospital or care
- ✓ **No council support** - The local council isn't paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of it back through tax
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

**Important Note:** Even if you earn over £60,000 and would have to pay back the Child Benefit through the High Income Child Benefit Charge, it's still worth registering for Child Benefit as it protects your National Insurance record and your State Pension entitlement.

Do you have any questions about the application process?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and studying full-time A-levels at sixth form college, which qualifies as approved non-advanced education. Alex is not working (under 24 hours/week requirement), does not receive benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with and is responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 17-year-old child Alex who is in full-time approved education (A-levels) at sixth form college.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and studying full-time A-levels at sixth form college, which qualifies as approved non-advanced education. Alex is not working (under 24 hours/week requirement), does not receive benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with and is responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 17-year-old child Alex who is in full-time approved education (A-levels) at sixth form college.
